wikidata_candidates.label: William Leeman
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The two records describe people from completely different eras. The Wikidata entity lived in the mid-19th century (1839–1859) and was an abolitionist involved in John Brown's raid. The card data describes an electronics engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (a 20th-century institution) specialized in digital circuitry and kinetic sculpture.

results.possibleIdentity: William G. Leeman was an electronics engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Specializing in electronics and digital circuitry, he was an associate of the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted collaborations between artists and the technical community. Leeman is documented in the E.A.T. archives for his work on a collaborative project with the artist Claudio Badal and fellow engineers Bob Allerot and Anthony 'Tony' Tedona. This collaboration, sometimes referred to as the 'Disc artist project,' involved the development and use of technical components such as a ring counter and a voice-activated microphone. Audio recordings of discussions related to this project (dating around 1966–1967) are preserved in the Experiments in Art and Technology records at the Getty Research Institute. Leeman resided in Matawan, New Jersey, and is listed as an alumnus of the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn (now the NYU Tandon School of Engineering).
